title: 👑 Elite Challenge ⭐⭐⭐⭐: Prepositions of Place and Time

questions.prompt:
Read: "Leila has a meeting at 10 o'clock on Thursday. She works in the city centre, at a law firm on King Street. After work, she goes to the gym."
Which sentence is TRUE?

options.text: Leila's meeting is in the morning on Friday.
Her office is at 10 King Street.
She works at a law firm in the city centre.
She goes into the gym before work.

questions.explanation: The text says she works "in the city centre, at a law firm" — (c) correctly restates both. The meeting is on Thursday (not Friday), her address is on King Street (not a number given), and she goes to the gym after work, not before.

options.text: I usually travel by bus on weekdays.
She arrived to Paris late at night.
The book is on the desk, next to the lamp.
We'll meet at the entrance at 6 pm.

questions.explanation: The error is (b): arrive is followed by in (for a city) or at (for a specific place), never arrive to — she arrived in Paris. (a) correctly uses on weekdays, (c) correctly uses on the desk (surface), and (d) correctly uses at the entrance and at 6 pm.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Please put your phone ___ your bag during the exam."

options.text: on
at
into
in

questions.explanation: A bag is a container, so we use in: put your phone in your bag. "into" would suggest actively dropping it inside (movement entering), which is less natural here for a simple resting state. "on" is for surfaces, and "at" marks a point.

questions.prompt: Complete: "The festival takes place ___ the main square ___ July every year."

options.text: in / at
at / on
in / in
at / in

questions.explanation: The main square is a specific named location → at the main square; a month takes in → in July. So at / in is correct. "in the main square" suggests an enclosed area, but a public square uses at. "on / in" and "in / at" don't match the location + month pattern.

options.text: She flew from Cairo to London last night.
He's on his way to the hospital.
We arrived in the airport at midnight.
The meeting room is on the second floor.

questions.explanation: The error is (c): an airport is a specific place, so use arrived at the airport, not "in the airport". "in" is for arriving in a city or country (arrived in London). (a) correctly uses from/to for a journey, (b) uses on his way to (fixed phrase), and (d) uses on the second floor (floor = surface level).

questions.prompt: Complete: "Could you put those files ___ the cabinet, please? ___ the second drawer, ___ the left."

options.text: into / In / on
on / At / in
at / On / at
in / In / at

questions.explanation: You put files into a cabinet (movement entering), they go in the second drawer (inside a compartment), and on the left (surface/side). "into / In / on" fits all three. "on / At / in" doesn't work — you don't put files on a cabinet here, and "At" is wrong for a drawer.
